```

issue_title: Peewee backend doesn't commit by default
issue_body:
I found this surprising, and it seems like a bug, but the Peewee backend doesn't save by default. It seems like it should default to committing, like the Django backend does. Here's a simple test case:

```python
from mixer.backend.peewee import mixer
from models import Foo
print(Foo.select().count())  # 0
mixer.blend(Foo)
print(Foo.select().count())  # Still 0, which was unexpected.
```

Doing this works just fine, but it's not documented anywhere that this is required:

```python
with mixer.ctx(commit=True):
    foo = mixer.blend(Foo)
```

issue_title: Interpolate env vars that look like integers as Python integers.
issue_body:
Discussion was started here: https://github.com/cdent/gabbi/issues/144#issuecomment-224988014

**my initial ask:**

> p.s. rather than opening another issue immediately, another quick question: Is it possible to source integers from environment variables in the YAML? I ask because I just tried to source poll delay and count from environ, but test failed complaining about strings vs. integers. I tried unquoting the value during shell export, but no dice. Worth opening a new issue?

**@cdent's reponse from #144:**
> The issue with strings and ints is because environment variables are always strings, see this block of code. In that block it would be possible to turn things that look like ints into its.
> However, poll and count, don't use substitutions so even if the environment was producing stuff it wouldn't get pulled, so some fixes would be needed in the poll handling.
> Yeah, if you could make a fresh issue, that would be great.

@cdent Please let me know if you meant to create a fresh issue for the poll/count substitutions and not for this.  If so, I'll close this issue and open one on that.

issue_title: ReactJS
issue_body:
> * This pattern provides for a reactive environment, similar to ReactJS
> * but with no build system/JSX, but at the cost of less efficiency. I prefer a Vanilla Javascript
> * to the complexity ReactJS introduces. ReactJS may be considered if performance becomes an issue
> * since it can render more efficently.

This is the comment I found in the gdbgui.js.

*Disclaimer: I haven't run this project locally, but the screencast is flickering and I am surprised that you have chosen to reimplement React with jQuery.*

What kind of complexity does React introduce in your opinion? Have you considered UMD builds (these are just good old "classic" way of packaging a module into a single .js file with globally exposed interfaces) of ReactJS? You may also like Preact and Inferno projects (they are extremely lightweight ReactJS implementations).

issue_title: Exception handling for WantRead and WantWrite
Unable to decorate a class method
issue_body:
Hello,

Here's a very simple example in an effort to decorate a class method using Pint,

``` python
from pint import UnitRegistry

ureg = UnitRegistry()
Q_ = ureg.Quantity

class Simple:
    def __init__(self):
        pass

    @ureg.wraps('m/s', (None, 'm/s'), True)
    def calculate(self, a, b):
        return a*b

if __name__ == "__main__":
    c = Simple().calculate(1, Q_(10, 'm/s'))
    print c
```

This code results in the below ValueError. 

``` python
Traceback (most recent call last):
   c = Simple().calculate(1, Q_(10, 'm/s'))
  File "build/bdist.macosx-10.11-intel/egg/pint/registry_helpers.py", line 167, in wrapper
  File "build/bdist.macosx-10.11-intel/egg/pint/registry_helpers.py", line 118, in _converter
ValueError: A wrapped function using strict=True requires quantity for all arguments with not None units. (error found for m / s, 1)
```

It seems to me that the issue here may be with class instances being passed to the pint decorator. Would anyone have a solution for fixing this?

Thanks
Saurabh

issue_title: ValueError: could not broadcast input array from shape (11,11) into shape (11)
issue_body:
I have the following code to fit a 2D gaussian:

```python
from symfit import Variable, Parameter, Fit, exp
import numpy as np


arr = np.genfromtxt('testarr.txt')


a = Parameter(name='a', value=8000)
b = Parameter(name='b', value=3000, min=100)
sig_x = Parameter(name='sig_x', value=2)
sig_y = Parameter(name='sig_y', value=1.5)
mu_x = Parameter(name='mu_x', value=0.5)
mu_y = Parameter(name='mu_y', value=0.5)
x_var = Variable(name='x_var')
y_var = Variable(name='y_var')
z_var = Variable(name='z_var')

model = {z_var: ((a*exp(-(((x_var-mu_x)**2/(2*sig_x**2)) + ((y_var-mu_y)**2 / (2*sig_y**2))))))+b}
shape = arr.shape

xv, yv = np.meshgrid(*[np.arange(1 - np.floor(x/2), x - np.floor(x/2) + 1) for x in shape])
fit = Fit(model, z_var=arr, y_var=yv, x_var=xv)
res = fit.execute()

print(res)
```

Which gives this error: 

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "C:\Program Files\JetBrains\PyCharm 2018.1\helpers\pydev\pydev_run_in_console.py", line 53, in run_file
    pydev_imports.execfile(file, globals, locals)  # execute the script
  File "C:\Program Files\JetBrains\PyCharm 2018.1\helpers\pydev\_pydev_imps\_pydev_execfile.py", line 18, in execfile
    exec(compile(contents+"\n", file, 'exec'), glob, loc)
  File "C:/Users/Smit/_processed_data/2018/20180419_psf/fitting.py", line 29, in <module>
    res = fit.execute()
  File "C:\Miniconda3\envs\py_main\lib\site-packages\symfit\core\fit.py", line 1364, in execute
    cov_matrix = self.covariance_matrix(dict(zip(self.model.params, minimizer_ans._popt)))
  File "C:\Miniconda3\envs\py_main\lib\site-packages\symfit\core\fit.py", line 850, in covariance_matrix
    return self._cov_mat_equal_lenghts(best_fit_params=best_fit_params)
  File "C:\Miniconda3\envs\py_main\lib\site-packages\symfit\core\fit.py", line 914, in _cov_mat_equal_lenghts
    jac = np.array(self.model.eval_jacobian(**kwargs))
  File "C:\Miniconda3\envs\py_main\lib\site-packages\symfit\core\fit.py", line 474, in eval_jacobian
    jac[idx] = np.array(jac[idx], dtype=float)
ValueError: could not broadcast input array from shape (11,11) into shape (11)
```

When I remove the constant `b` from the model, it does run. 
```
 model = {z_var: ((a*exp(-(((x_var-mu_x)**2/(2*sig_x**2)) + ((y_var-mu_y)**2 / (2*sig_y**2))))))}
``` 

However, I'd very much like to have this `b` there. I'm aware I have a surplus of brackets, but for brackets goes, the more the better. 

I'm using symfit 0.4.0 and numpy 1.13.1, which is the latest version distributed by conda. With numpy 1.14.2 the bug persists.

issue_title: Reverse-proxy authentication
issue_body:
Hello !
Thanks for this grate project. And I have one request for you.
I need to authenticate my users against LDAP. As wsgidav does not have this middleware, I use my nginx configuration to do it before proxying to wsgidav. In short :
user ---> nginx (with ldap authentication) ---> uwsgi running wsgidav
Nginx filters well the users, but wsgidav does not know about them, even if the REMOTE_USER variable is passed.
As I would like to restrict certain folders to a few users, wsgidav needs to know them, at least their username, which is passed with REMOTE_USER. And I do not want to either have a second authentication inside wsgidav nor can i remove the ldap authentication from nginx and enter manually each user/password in the wsgidav configuration.
I think #11 was talking about it, but I seek in the configuration and it seems that scenario is not possible yet.
Adding this possibility really would be great.

issue_title: Question: yas3fs limitations when used for non-AWS or hybrid scenarios
issue_body:
Hi,

I am currently using "Azure Files" - an SMB-based solution to share files between a number of servers. Everything is working great, except I can only attach this storage to Azure servers (and they have to be in one region). Now, we have decided to expand our network to other VPS providers, such as AWS.
 
Looking for a solution that would allow us to share files between all of our servers (wherever they are hosted), I have stumbled upon yas3fs. So the plan is to store all files in S3, and mount the share to all servers via yas3fs.

Thus, I wanted to ask if there are any limitations to mounting/using yas3fs in non-AWS environments (e.g. SQS not working, etc) prior to getting in too deep with this plan.

Thanks in advance.

issue_title: libjpeg: Use TRUE instead of true
issue_body:
There are a lot of places in the source code where `true` is being used. While this is widely acknowledged and works, it would be a good idea to use TRUE. [This](https://travis-ci.org/AbdealiJK/file-metadata/builds/135429131#L687) travis build fails saying `cannot convert bool to boolean for ...`

The same issue was found in [lammps](https://sourceforge.net/p/lammps/mailman/message/32775346/) and they were able to fix it by using TRUE and Axel Kohlmeyer says:

> i think that is more likely due to the compiler.
> in any case, the suggested fix is correct and compatible with all
> compiler variants that i could test.

The following script is running without problem

    import plyplus

    start: a another_a;

    grammar = plyplus.Grammar("""
    start: a another_a;

    a: 'a';
    another_a: '[a]';

issue_title: Default keyring is insecure
issue_body:
Just using the default from the keyring module defaults to a plaintext keyring file, which is very insecure. Either default to the system keyring, an otherwise encrypted keyring, or just fail with an error if you can't actually provide a secure keyring.

Making plaintext the default will just lead users to think they are secure when their passwords are all dumped to the disk in plain text, which is insane.

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/kang/python-keyring-lib/issue/117
- Originally reported by: Stavros Korokithakis
- Originally created at: 2013-09-27T14:11:44.531

issue_title: Correlated Sequence bug?
issue_body:
copy & paste example from docs:
```python
from durable.lang import *

with ruleset('risk'):
    @when_all(c.first << m.amount > 10,
              c.second << m.amount > c.first.amount * 2,
              c.third << m.amount > (c.first.amount + c.second.amount) / 2)
    def detected(c):
        print('fraud detected -> {0}'.format(c.first.amount))
        print('               -> {0}'.format(c.second.amount))
        print('               -> {0}'.format(c.third.amount))
        
    @when_start
    def start(host):
        host.post('risk', { 'amount': 50 })
        host.post('risk', { 'amount': 200 })
        host.post('risk', { 'amount': 300 })

run_all()
```
running it:
```bash
$ python fraud.py 
 * Running on http://127.0.0.1:5000/ (Press CTRL+C to quit)
fraud detected -> 50
               -> 200
               -> 200
^C
```
This happens for any value instead of 300 in the last (third) post, or even without posting the third value at all. When i strip whole `@when_start` block and post events manually it indeed triggers just after sending second event:
```
$ curl -H "content-type: application/json" -X POST -d '{"amount": 50}' http://localhost:5000/risk/events
$ curl -H "content-type: application/json" -X POST -d '{"amount": 200}' http://localhost:5000/risk/events
```
result:
```
$ python fraud_modified.py 
 * Running on http://127.0.0.1:5000/ (Press CTRL+C to quit)
127.0.0.1 - - [02/Jun/2018 08:11:52] "POST /risk/events HTTP/1.1" 200 -
127.0.0.1 - - [02/Jun/2018 08:12:01] "POST /risk/events HTTP/1.1" 200 -
fraud detected -> 50
               -> 200
               -> 200
```

issue_title: Combo giving warning when select_default() is used
issue_body:
Hi, first of all I would like to thank you for making this wrapper, it's nice and easy to use and the 'warning' provided in the console are of great help in learning to use it.

I could be wrong but I believe there is a bug in the code, possibly from adjusting a previous situation (https://github.com/lawsie/guizero/issues/206). When .select_default() is used, the Combo performs its work perfectly but it looks like it doesn't return the 'True' flag from its successful operation and this warning is sent to the console...
------------------------------------------------------------
*** GUIZERO WARNING ***
[Combo] object with options  ['choice1', 'choice2']
Unable to select default option as the Combo is empty
------------------------------------------------------------
...but clearly the list isn't empty.

I think I have located the issue in Combo.py:
def _set_option_by_index(self, index):
        """
        Sets a single option in the Combo by its index, returning True if it was able too.
        """
        if index < len(self._options) - 1:
            self._selected.set(self._options[index])
**#Missing?==>return True;**
        else:
            return False


FYI I am using version 0.5.3.

I hope this helps.

Thanks.

issue_title: Provide Average based on number of attempts over time
issue_body:
This is a feature req. that I just thought of. I've been doing some testing of proxy servers and have used to httpstat to gain insight into how long each part of the connection process takes. To make httpstat even more adept to performance based testing like this, it would be exceptionally awesome if it had the option for making x number of requests over y timeframe and providing those results in a similar drawing to how it currently does i.e. TLS Handshake avg, Server Processing avg, etc..

Just a thought ;)

    def drawOn(self, canv, x, y, _sW=0):
        pass
```
Maybe it is sufficient to just provide a SvgReader() class, that gives the freedom to implement this in a flexible way...

The wrap() can be overloaded, whereas the wrapOn() method should not be touched.

I found an example somewhere that explains what it could look like, only that this shows how to embed a PDF file:
```
from reportlab.platypus import Flowable
from pdfrw import PdfReader,PdfDict
from pdfrw.buildxobj import pagexobj
from pdfrw.toreportlab import makerl

class PdfAsset(Flowable):
    def __init__(self,fname,width=None,height=None,kind='direct'):

        self.page = PdfReader(fname=fname, decompress=False).pages[0]
        self.xobj = pagexobj(self.page)

        self.imageWidth = width
        self.imageHeight = height
        x1, y1, x2, y2 = self.xobj.BBox

        self._w, self._h = x2 - x1, y2 - y1
        if not self.imageWidth:
            self.imageWidth = self._w
        if not self.imageHeight:
            self.imageHeight = self._h
        self.__ratio = float(self.imageWidth)/self.imageHeight
        if kind in ['direct','absolute'] or width==None or height==None:
            self.drawWidth = width or self.imageWidth
            self.drawHeight = height or self.imageHeight
        elif kind in ['bound','proportional']:
            factor = min(float(width)/self._w,float(height)/self._h)
            self.drawWidth = self._w*factor
            self.drawHeight = self._h*factor

    def wrap(self, width, height):
        return self.imageWidth, self.imageHeight

    def drawOn(self, canv, x, y, _sW=0):
        if _sW > 0 and hasattr(self, 'hAlign'):
            a = self.hAlign
            if a in ('CENTER', 'CENTRE', TA_CENTER):
                x += 0.5*_sW
            elif a in ('RIGHT', TA_RIGHT):
                x += _sW
            elif a not in ('LEFT', TA_LEFT):
                raise ValueError("Bad hAlign value " + str(a))
        canv.saveState()
        img = self.xobj
        if isinstance(img, PdfDict):
            xscale = self.imageWidth / img.BBox[2]
            yscale = self.imageHeight / img.BBox[3]
            canv.translate(x, y)
            canv.scale(xscale, yscale)
            canv.doForm(makerl(canv, img))
        else:
            #canv.drawInlineImage(img, x, y-self.imageHeight, self.imageWidth, self.imageHeight)
            canv.drawImage(img, x, y, self.imageWidth, self.imageHeight)
        canv.restoreState()
```

issue_title: 问一下，API文档在哪里
Created Is validator, is it useful for creating a PR?
Extracting public key off of v3 SSL Certificate
issue_body:
Hi Andrey,

I'd like to make an enhancement suggestion to the ```strip_pem_header``` procedure in signxml/util/\_\_init\_\_.py.
Currently the code is expecting a header and footer which are skipped. However v3 certificates might include additional information before the header. As a result the signature X509Certificate content is loaded with additional data that doesn't belong there, producing an invalid signature.

I'm suggesting replacing:
```
def strip_pem_header_old(cert):
    bare_base64_cert = ""
    for line in ensure_str(cert).splitlines():
        if line != PEM_HEADER and line != PEM_FOOTER:
            bare_base64_cert += line
    return bare_base64_cert
```
with:
```
def strip_pem_header(cert):
    bare_base64_cert = ""
    head = cert.index(PEM_HEADER)
    if head < 0:
        raise Exception("Certificate PEM Header Not Found")
    head += len(PEM_HEADER)
    tail = cert.index(PEM_FOOTER, head)
    if tail < head:
        raise Exception("Certificate PEM Footer Not Found")
    return cert[head:tail].strip()
```

Regards,
Vic.

issue_title: Jedi import statement autocompletion removes working directory from sys.path
issue_body:
#### Paste the following code into an interactive python REPL:

```python
import jedi
import sys


source = 'import '
j = jedi.Interpreter(source, [locals()])

assert '' in sys.path
_ = j.completions()
assert '' in sys.path
```

In Jedi=0.12.0, the completion will remove the current directory (`''`) from sys.path. Jedi<0.12 will not exhibit this behaviour.

#### The code responsible can be found here:
https://github.com/davidhalter/jedi/blob/e96ebbe88f428775aa4dd16cbc9e7c3ee850e44d/jedi/api/project.py#L80-L93

#### A stacktrace inserted at that point will show:
<details><summary>Trace:</summary>

```
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "C:\Python36\lib\site-packages\jedi\api\__init__.py", line 176, in completions
    completions = completion.completions()
  File "C:\Python36\lib\site-packages\jedi\api\completion.py", line 97, in completions
    completion_names = self._get_context_completions()
  File "C:\Python36\lib\site-packages\jedi\api\completion.py", line 197, in _get_context_completions
    only_modules=only_modules,
  File "C:\Python36\lib\site-packages\jedi\api\completion.py", line 275, in _get_importer_names
    retval = i.completion_names(self._evaluator, only_modules=only_modules)
  File "C:\Python36\lib\site-packages\jedi\evaluate\imports.py", line 461, in completion_names
    names += self._get_module_names()
  File "C:\Python36\lib\site-packages\jedi\evaluate\imports.py", line 398, in _get_module_names
    search_path = self.sys_path_with_modifications()
  File "C:\Python36\lib\site-packages\jedi\evaluate\imports.py", line 266, in sys_path_with_modifications
    sys_path_mod = self._evaluator.get_sys_path()
  File "C:\Python36\lib\site-packages\jedi\evaluate\__init__.py", line 125, in get_sys_path
    retval = self.project._get_sys_path(self, environment=self.environment)
  File "C:\Python36\lib\site-packages\jedi\evaluate\cache.py", line 40, in wrapper
    rv = function(obj, *args, **kwargs)
  File "C:\Python36\lib\site-packages\jedi\api\project.py", line 120, in _get_sys_path
    sys_path = list(self._get_base_sys_path(environment))
  File "C:\Python36\lib\site-packages\jedi\api\project.py", line 101, in _get_base_sys_path
    traceback.print_stack()
```
</details>

#### This behaviour will cause confusion / bugs as seen here:
https://github.com/jonathanslenders/ptpython/issues/248
https://github.com/jonathanslenders/ptpython/issues/246

#### I see two Problems:
1. Why is the code for removing `''` necessary?
2. I don't think Jedi completions should mutate the namespace argument at all.

issue_title: Multi-language bot
issue_body:
Hi Nick! 

I'm trying to make my bot a multi-language one.
So far, if I add the gettext install to __init__, all messages come in the same language:
```
import os
import locale
(...)
localedir = os.path.join(os.path.abspath(os.path.dirname(__file__)), 'lang')
esp = gettext.translation('int.py', localedir, languages=['es'])
eng = gettext.translation('int.py', localedir, languages=['en'])
(...)
class MyBot(telepot.helper.ChatHandler):
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super(MyBot, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        eng.install()
(...)
bot = telepot.DelegatorBot(BOT_TOKEN, [
    include_callback_query_chat_id(
        pave_event_space())(
            per_chat_id(), create_open, MyBot, timeout=15
    ),
])

```

I'd like to choose a language based on the user preferences. For instance, if from-id is X, then spanish, else english.

Of course, in the __init__ I don't have the message values, so I can't use them to make de decision. But if I call "install()" in "on_message" function, I'll be calling "install()" way more than I need. Optimally I'd like to call "install()" once per Delegator session.

Is there a place where I can call gettext install() only once per session?

Sorry if I wasn't completely clear... If I wasn't, I'll try to elaborate.

Thanks in advance for your attention, and of course thank you very much for this very useful framework!

issue_title: Consumer iterator sometimes stops consuming after change in assignment
issue_body:
After a change in assignment the consumer iterator sometimes seems to stop consuming messages. No luck in isolating why yet. It's intermittent, but seems to be quite frequent. I haven't seen a consumer recover from this - each time it seems to hang indefinitely. I've confirmed the consumer group is behind on the partitions assigned to the consumer in each case, so there should be messages for it to consume. The consumer has also often been consuming fine until a rebalance. 

I've only noticed it with the iterator, not `poll()`, and since upgrading to 1.4.1. It's possible it's gone unnoticed in other situations, however.

Some example logs:
```
[2018-02-19 23:01:57,299] kafka.coordinator.WARNING some-group-heartbeat base._handle_heartbeat_response base.py:810 Heartbeat failed for group some-group because it is rebalancing
[2018-02-19 23:01:57,805] kafka.coordinator.consumer.INFO MainThread consumer._on_join_prepare consumer.py:341 Revoking previously assigned partitions {TopicPartition(topic='some-topic', partition=5), TopicPartition(topic='some-topic', partition=3), TopicPartition(topic='some-topic', partition=4)} for group some-group
[2018-02-19 23:01:57,805] kafka.coordinator.INFO MainThread base._send_join_group_request base.py:430 (Re-)joining group some-group
[2018-02-19 23:01:58,275] kafka.coordinator.INFO MainThread base._handle_join_group_response base.py:500 Elected group leader -- performing partition assignments using range
[2018-02-19 23:01:58,330] kafka.coordinator.INFO MainThread base._handle_join_success base.py:329 Successfully joined group some-group with generation 463
[2018-02-19 23:01:58,331] kafka.consumer.subscription_state.INFO MainThread subscription_state.assign_from_subscribed subscription_state.py:257 Updated partition assignment: [TopicPartition(topic='some-topic', partition=2), TopicPartition(topic='some-topic', partition=3)]
[2018-02-19 23:01:58,331] kafka.coordinator.consumer.INFO MainThread consumer._on_join_complete consumer.py:238 Setting newly assigned partitions {TopicPartition(topic='some-topic', partition=3), TopicPartition(topic='some-topic', partition=2)} for group some-group

Have you considered adding a new role in kafka-python such as "metadata-tools" ?

issue_title: Dealing with Nulldata - current issues
issue_body:
Currently pycoin does deal with nulldata to some degree (I submitted the original PR), but there are a few problems:

1. Nulldata over 40 bytes is not matched agains the nulldata template ([ref](https://github.com/richardkiss/pycoin/blob/master/pycoin/tx/pay_to/ScriptType.py#L72)), however, nulldata up to 80 bytes is considered standard (as of bitcoin core 0.12 IIRC)
2. Even though nulldata txs over 40 or 80 bytes are non-standard, they are still nulldata outputs
3. Some nulldata is improperly formatted ([example](https://www.blocktrail.com/BTC/tx/82da9ffc424ccd20b8f9ba47e7f5c3bf661e1b3a0e6bfda76296df8921e63a2b#tx_messages)), even though it decodes to something somewhat meaningful (though it's hard to tell how much of the non-ascii bytes are meaningful)

I noticed this because 3) was throwing errors in my [nulldata-api-thing](http://opreturn.ninja/#main). Called via `pycoin.tx.pay_to.__init__.script_obj_from_script`, the error is `unexpected end of data when literal expected, <class 'pycoin.tx.script.ScriptError'>`. The relevant traceback is included below. Particularly the error is in the lines:

`opcode1, data1, pc1 = tools.get_opcode(script, pc1)`

`raise ScriptError("unexpected end of data when literal expected")`

## Suggested Solutions

For 1), the obvious solution is to bump the length parameter to 80 bytes instead of 40.

For 2), I think we should actually drop the length check all together. Two possible things could happen (AFAIK). The first is that we have a well-encoded nulldata entry (regardless of length) and the second is that we have a badly encoded entry as in 3). Badly encoded entries should be dealt with separately I feel. Additionally I think we should probably accept _any_ well encoded nulldata txout, regardless of whether it's standard or not. This is also rather more future compatible.

For 3), I don't think we should deal with this in the ScriptNulldata class and template. It would require extensive changes to the script parser because it currently expects nulldata and other variable length data to be length encoded. Rather, we _could_ create another script type called ScriptRawNulldata or something, which is very similar to ScriptNulldata, but just dumps back _everything_ after the OP_RETURN. I don't know how well this would fly with the template matching engine, or if it's possible.

Part of me wants to argue that they aren't actually Nulldata outputs at all; since nulldatas are by definition length encoded [citation needed]. Rather, they're just provably prunable outputs.

Additionally, bitcoind doesn't decode them, it just returns: `"asm": "OP_RETURN [error]",`.

#### Maybe a better way to deal with 3)

A second way to deal with 3) would be to catch the ScriptError in ScriptNulldata and set the nulldata to `[error]` - the output would then match bitcoind and the hex is available in the script anyway, so it's still accessible.

## What I'm going to do

I'm going to create and submit a PR to fix 1 and 2 by removing the length check. Since (I think) the template matching engine will pick up any valid length encoded nulldata this should have no side effects besides some nulldatas being parsable when they weren't previously.

#### Traceback from point 3)

```
Oct 12 00:22:04 bitcoin-node-2 bash[22792]:   File "/home/bitcoin/src/opreturn-ninja/opreturnninja/models.py", line 91, in merge_nulldatas_from_block_obj                           Oct 12 00:22:04 bitcoin-node-2 bash[22792]:     script_object = script_obj_from_script(tx_out.script)                                                                               Oct 12 00:22:04 bitcoin-node-2 bash[22792]:   File "/home/bitcoin/.venv/opreturn/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pycoin/tx/pay_to/__init__.py", line 34, in script_obj_from_script
Oct 12 00:22:04 bitcoin-node-2 bash[22792]:     st = sc.from_script(script)
Oct 12 00:22:04 bitcoin-node-2 bash[22792]:   File "/home/bitcoin/.venv/opreturn/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pycoin/tx/pay_to/ScriptNulldata.py", line 17, in from_script
Oct 12 00:22:04 bitcoin-node-2 bash[22792]:     r = cls.match(script)

issue_title: Add request timeouts
issue_body:
- Add a user-configurable request timeout (e.g. 5, 10, 15, 30 seconds) for `connection` requests.
- If timeout has expired, the modal spinner will be hidden and a popup will be shown, asking the user to wait until the request has finished, or to cancel the request.
- Since there's no way to "cancel" a request, the latter option will simply discard any response arriving later; alternatively, a reconnect may be performed. This is to prevent unexpected updates, as they now occur for example when browsing large collections.
- When waiting for the request to finish, the timeout may be re-applied, e.g. in case the response got "lost" somehow.

issue_title: modify_data() and then performing a recv() on the same bus will not see the data change.
issue_body:
1. Start a task running cyclic messages on a can interface (vcan0)
    **msg = can.Message(arbitration_id=0x123, data=[0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0], extended_id=False)**
    **task = can.send_periodic('vcan0', msg, 0.20)**

2. Instantiate a bus on the same interface as running task
     **bus = can.interface.Bus(bustype='socketcan_native', channel='vcan0')**

3. Modify data associated with running task on (vcan0)
     **msg.data[0] = 0x99**
     **task.modify_data(msg)**

4.  Perform a recv() on the bus instantiated in step 2.
      **read_msg = bus.recv()**

The read_msg does not see the change performed by the modify task in step 3.  In order for me to see the change, I would have to perform step 2 after step 3.  Any subsequent modify_data calls I will have to reinstantiate a bus and then perform a recv() in order to see the change.

Is this working as intended?

---

Hello,
I'm trying to test a Denial of Service (DoS) attack such that legitimate messages cannot be received because the network is flooded with high priority IDs. 

My experiment consists of two programs, each of which simulates an ECU:

ECU1: sends periodic messages with the ID 0x009.
It also has 0x009 as a filter in order to receive it  to confirm other ECUs also received it.

ECU2: Malicious program that sends a very large number of 0x008 IDs whenever a 0x009 ID is detected in the can bus.

My problem is this: ECU1 always receives 0x009 ID, even though the network is flooded with 0x008 ID that intended to prevent such successful transmission!

Any help would be greatly appreciated.

issue_title: Wrong completions if argparse.py in module
issue_body:
Hi,

## Versions:
-  argcomplete from yesterday (pip)
- argparse 1.3.0 from https://pypi.python.org/pypi/argparse

For my project I have to support old versions of python, therefore I ship a local argparse.py.

### Preparation
```
$ mkdir mytest
# for the version that works:
$ cp /somewhere/argparse-1.3.0/argparse.py .
# for the version that does not work:
$ cp /somewhere/argparse-1.3.0/argparse.py ./mytest
```

### main.py
Uncomment the "works" version or any of the "does not work" versions, and
```
#!/usr/bin/env python2

#works:
# import argparse as margparse
#does not work
# import mytest.argparse as margparse
# from mytest import argparse as margparse
import argcomplete
import sys

p = margparse.ArgumentParser()
s = p.add_subparsers()

l = s.add_parser('list', aliases=['l'])
se = s.add_parser('set', aliases=['s'])

l.add_argument('--foo-list')
se.add_argument('--foo-set')

argcomplete.autocomplete(p)

print p.parse_args(sys.argv[1:])
```

## OK
If main.py and argparse.py are in the same directory, everything is fine:
```
$ ./main.py set -<tab><tab>
--foo-set  -h         --help
$ ./main.py list -<tab><tab>
--foo-list  -h          --help
```
## Strange behavior
If I try to import argparse from a module/subdirectory, in my case "mytest", the completion returns wrong results:
```
$ ./main.py list <tab><tab>
-h      --help  l       list    s       set
$ ./main.py list -<tab><tab>
-h      --help
```

issue_title: problems with mixed optional and positional arguments
issue_body:
With this test program:

```
#!/usr/bin/env python
import argparse
import argcomplete
def c_test(prefix, parsed_args, **kwargs):
    return [ "name1", "name2" ]
parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument("name").completer = c_test
exc = parser.add_mutually_exclusive_group()
exc.add_argument("--get", action="store_true")
exc.add_argument("--set", action="store_true")
argcomplete.autocomplete(parser)
args = parser.parse_args()
```

Usage is listed as:

```
usage: test.py [-h] [--get | --set] name
```

But optional arguments aren't suggested as completions, and completions for `name` are listed even after a `name` has been supplied.

Expected:

```
$ ./test.py <tab>
-h --get --set name1 name2
$ ./test.py --<tab>
--get --set
$ ./test.py --get <tab>
-h name1 name2
$ ./test.py --get name1 <tab>
-h
```

Actual:

```
$ ./test.py <tab>
name1 name2
$ ./test.py --<tab>
(nothing)
$ ./test.py --get <tab>
name1 name2
$ ./test.py --get name1 <tab>
name1 name2
```

issue_title: @ajax decorator does not set http error code in JSONResponse
issue_body:
When raising an exception in a function decorated with @ajax, the http status code is not set according to the error code:

HTTP status is 200 (OK):
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/839540/7503676/d2d3f9ea-f444-11e4-8326-44a2b9963163.png)

However, the JSON-content is set correctly:
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/839540/7503727/1bfda7e2-f445-11e4-9f4b-3936ef7c2966.png)

This can be fixed by changing response.py as follows (added status=data['status']):

```python
class JSONResponse(HttpResponse):
    """
    Return a JSON serialized HTTP response
    """

    def __init__(self, data, *args, **kwargs):
        """
        This returns a object that we send as json content using
        utils.serialize_to_json, that is a wrapper to json.dumps
        method using a custom class to handle models and querysets. Put your
        options to serialize_to_json in kwargs, other options are used by
        response.
        """
        if not 'sort_keys' in kwargs:
            kwargs['sort_keys'] = settings.DEBUG

        super(JSONResponse, self).__init__(
            status=data['status'],
            content=serialize_to_json(data, *args, **kwargs),
            content_type='application/json'
        )
```

Result:
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/839540/7503782/709cfd48-f445-11e4-8e72-6e8a1a1f8c3c.png)

issue_title: Formats indirectly with tags a'la Tk
issue_body:
After trying several ways to mitigate the pain of lots of fiddly formatting work with XLSXwriter, I've been rather happy with the concept of tags after the fashion of Tk. Essentially, I'd like this interface:

	sheet.write(row, col, item, *tags)

The basic idea is to maintain a dictionary of tag definitions, like so:

	def define_tag(self, tag, **options):
		tag = tag.strip()
		assert len(tag) and ' ' not in tag
		assert tag not in self.tag_definitions
		self.tag_definitions[tag] = _fix_fmt(options)

The shortcut options like "border" get broken into their constituent parts with a relatively unsurprising function called `fix_fmt`. Its definition is left as an exercise to the reader.

Later, when it comes time to convert a bag of tags into an appropriate formatting object, I have this method:

	def render_format(self, *tags):
		"Return the format corresponding to the argument tags (or lists of them) creating it if necessary."
		# Normalize the arguments, which are each python-lists of tags: later duplicates win.
		tags = ' '.join(reversed(uniq(reversed(_fixup(*tags)))))
		
		if tags not in self.format_cache:
			options = {}
			for t in tags.split(): options.update(self.tag_definitions[t])
			self.format_cache[tags] = self.workbook.add_format(options)
		return self.format_cache[tags]
	
You do need a couple utility functions:

	def _fixup(*args): return sum((a.split() if isinstance(a, str) else _fixup(*a) for a in args), [])
	def uniq(them): seen = set(); return [x for x in them if x not in seen and not seen.add(x)]

You could even extend this to support tagging data types:

	def tag_type(self, type, *tags):
		if type not in self.type_tags: self.type_tags[type] = []
		self.type_tags[type].extend(tags)
		
	def type_tags_for(self, item): return sum((self.type_tags[k] for k in self.type_tags if isinstance(item, k)), [])

	def write(self, row, col, item, *tags): self.sheet.write(row, col, item, self.render_format(self.type_tags_for(item), *tags))

and perhaps during initialization, you perform:
	
	workbook.define_tag('dmy', num_format = 'dd-mmm-yyyy')
	workbook.tag_type(datetime.date, 'dmy')

Now all your dates are sensibly formatted as dates *along with whatever other formatting, without you having to think about it.*

issue_title: Feature Request: Conditional Formatting Rows in Pandas
issue_body: ## Feature request Is there a solution that provides dynamic row conditional formatting based on a keyword in a particular column while dynamically generating a pandas dataframe into xlsxWriter? I've tried many different processes, but there are issues I keep running into when trying to populate a dynamically generated a pandas dataframe in xlsxwriter. Here is a some insights I've noticed while trying to generate a different background color when the keyword 'Total' exists in the A column using a Pandas DataFrame. - When formatting a row, I lose the column formatting. For example, if I use `format6 = workbook.add_format({'bold':True,'bottom':True,'top':True})` for the column and `format5 = workbook.add_format({'bg_color': '59955C','font_color':'white'})` for a row, I lose the column formatting. - The below code works for a Pandas Dataframe, but it is hard coded and does not consider the dynamically generated rows, as the rows can change based on what the dataframe pulls. ```python worksheet.conditional_format('$A$35:$K$35', {'type' : 'formula', 'criteria': '=$A35="Total"', 'format' : format5}) ``` - While formulas such as the below work for isolating the conditional formatting to one cell, I've not found a good solution for row based conditional formatting. ```python worksheet.conditional_format(color_range, {'type': 'text', 'criteria': 'containsText', 'value':'Total', 'format': format5}) ``` Thanks!

issue_title: Query result dictionary order lost
issue_body:
```
aql = """FOR x IN Link
              FILTER CONTAINS(x._from, "Core")
              SORT x._from, x._to, x.Port1
              RETURN {[x._from]:x.Port1, [x._to]:x.Port2}"""
result = self.aql_query(aql)
print(result.result[0])
```

 ```
def aql_query(self, aql, rawResults=True, batchSize=100):
   query_result = self.db.AQLQuery(aql, rawResults=rawResults, batchSize=batchSize)
   return query_result

```
Link is a collection of edges in a topology. 
Example of a document in Link:{"Port1":"Port2","Port2":"NM_Port1","_from":"Router/Core1","_to":"Router/Edge2","name":"Core1Edge2"}

Expected response while running pyArango code:
[{'Router/Core1': 'Port1', 'Router/Edge1': 'NM_Port1'}, {'Router/Core1': 'Port2', 'Router/Edge2': 'NM_Port1'}]

Received response while running pyArango code:
[{'Router/Edge1': 'NM_Port1', 'Router/Core1': 'Port1'}, {'Router/Edge2': 'NM_Port1', 'Router/Core1': 'Port2'}]

Expected and received response while running the query in the arango interface:
[{'Router/Core1': 'Port1', 'Router/Edge1': 'NM_Port1'}, {'Router/Core1': 'Port2', 'Router/Edge2': 'NM_Port1'}]

My understanding is - when python gets the query result, it does not care about the order in which the dictionaries are in the result. So even thought the sort function is present in the arango query, it is being overwritten. 

I wonder if something can be done about this issue!

issue_title: release 0.10.0
issue_body:
This will include the complete protocol rewrite, whose main user-visible feature is the ability for me to bounce the relay server while a client is waiting for their peer to connect, without losing the connection (and burning that wormhole code). The internal API is not yet stable, but I'm much happier with this one than what was in 0.9.x .

Our release is blocked on landing #171, which makes sure that the client bails if the first attempt to connect to the relay has failed. This is what 0.9.x did too, but the new state machines in trunk would silently attempt to reconnect forever, which doesn't seem like a great user experience. We can't land #171 until Twisted-17.5 is released, since it depends upon a new `twisted.application.internet.clientService` API that is in Twisted trunk but not yet in a release.

We also want #170, but that should be trivial.

issue_title: Binary PSO cost problem
issue_body:
* PySwarms version: 0.1.6a


### Description

"The cost doesn't seem to monotonically decrease from one iteration to the next.  It seems like the cost that should be returned each iteration as the best cost is the one that is the historically best across all particles, yet on some iterations the cost seems to jump up, and the final cost when the algorithm completes isn't the minimum cost it encountered and returned in previous iterations. "

"When I reduce the inertia, the problem reduces (but it's still there).  That behavior suggests to me that the global best position is computed from only particles' current positions and not their past positions.  That way, as particles become more likely to explore, they are more likely to move into and out of good solutions.  Could that be what's going on?  That the global / social best positions are computed only on the current iteration and not on the history of found solutions?"

### What I Did

def rmse_func(positions):

issue_title: Set more to x number of pages?
issue_body:
Currently on starting, the kernel does `set more off`. If someone loads a 1 million row dataset and types `list`, 1 million rows of the data will be printed on the screen or until the user `KeyboardInterrupt`s. This is probably undesirable, as some users won't know to `Ctrl-C`, and it's slightly different on Hydrogen and Jupyter Notebook.

So probably set `set more 10` or something at the beginning, and then also `expect` for `\r\n--more--`. Then if you hit that, automatically press `q` and tell user that output was shortened by `more`.

Note that `set more 1` doesn't work for shell commands. `!yes` runs forever.

issue_title: CoxPH and strata base survival functions
issue_body:
Perhaps I've missed something, but it seems that when using the strata option in CoxPHFitter's fit() method, we don't get different base hazards output for each strata.

Using R's survival library, if we apply 'strata' in coxph() to train a model and call the result "fit",  then the results of basehaz(fit) have n-many baseline hazard sets where n is the number of values in the strata. On the other hand, 'base_cum_hazard' in Lifelines seems to output only one strata.

For example, if a timeline has 365 points and the strata variable has 3 values, then basehaz(fit) will have 3*365 points whereas 'base_cum_hazard' will only have 365 points.

The feature coefficients (and their stats, e.g. se and concordance) are the same in R and Lifelines - assuming that normalize=False in the latter. It's just the baseline hazards I'm curious about.

issue_title: Color continuation in syslog
issue_body:
Consider the following snippet that simply creates a logger with a colourful formatter and logs a few messages:

```python
import sys
import logging
import logging.handlers
import colorlog


# create the logger with the appropriate name
logger = logging.getLogger(name="somelogger")
# set logger's log-level
logger.setLevel("DEBUG")

# assemble the logging format
fmt = 'someproject: %(process)d %(processName)s %(asctime)-15s %(levelname)-8s %(name)-10s %(message)s'

# create a colourful formatter
formatter_w_color = colorlog.ColoredFormatter(
    fmt="%(log_color)s" + fmt + %(reset)s,
    datefmt="%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ",
    reset=True,
    log_colors={
        'DEBUG': 'blue',
        'INFO': 'green',
        'WARNING': 'yellow',
        'ERROR': 'red',
        'CRITICAL': 'red,bg_white',
    },
    secondary_log_colors={},
    style='%'
)

handler_stdout = logging.StreamHandler(sys.stdout)
handler_stdout.setFormatter(formatter_w_color)
logger.addHandler(handler_stdout)

handler_syslog = logging.handlers.SysLogHandler(address="/dev/log")
handler_syslog.setFormatter(formatter_w_color)
logger.addHandler(handler_syslog)


logger.debug("debug")
logger.info("debug")
logger.warning("debug")
logger.error("debug")
logger.critical("debug")
```

The stdout and syslog can also be seen in the following image:

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/272419/22959188/5a858ab8-f388-11e6-978a-e03081362167.png)

As you can see it all looks good in `stdout` but in `syslog` there's a `#033[0m` that causes the colour to bleed into the next line until the next colour kicks in.

Am I doing sth inordinately stupid or is this to be expected from a syslog handler?

issue_title: Exception is raised when init_app() is called more than once
issue_body:
The culprit is this line of code in init_app():
```
self.add_resource(self.swagger_view(), '/swagger.json', endpoint='specs', doc=False)
```
The self.swagger_view() method returns a new class each time. And, because of that, the code in the following if-block gets executed: https://github.com/flask-restful/flask-restful/blob/0.3.1/flask_restful/__init__.py#L391

This is very inconvenient for me, as because of this issue I can't create more than one class for my functional tests: for each such class, I must call my application factory, which, in turn, imports my Api instance and calls init_app() on it.

The fastest solution that I see right now is to just make the addition of the specs optional via a new kwarg. I can create a pull request for this.

issue_title: Deleting DNS records that are associated with a fixed address
issue_body:
According to API doc, “ipv4address” search cannot be used by cloud api because this object belongs to Host Record creation.

> “ipv4address” object has restriction “The object cannot be managed on Cloud Platform members”

That is why host record worked but not fixedaddress.

Here is the API test:

curl -k1 -u cloud:cloud -H "content-type:application/json" -w "\nThe Response Code:%{http_code}\n" https://10.40.240.101/wapi/v2.3/ipv4address?ip_address=99.90.93.10
{ "Error": "AdmConProtoError: Operation read not allowed for ipv4address on Cloud Platform member in this version",
  "code": "Client.Ibap.Proto",
  "text": "Operation read not allowed for ipv4address on Cloud Platform member in this version"
}
The Response Code:400

But searching with fixedaddress works:

curl -k1 -u cloud:cloud -H "content-type:application/json" -w "\nThe Response Code:%{http_code}\n" https://10.40.240.101/wapi/v2.3/fixedaddress?ipv4addr=99.90.93.10
[
    {
        "_ref": "fixedaddress/ZG5zLmZpeGVkX2FkZHJlc3MkOTkuOTAuOTMuMTAuMi4u:99.90.93.10/cloud_view",
        "ipv4addr": "99.90.93.10",
        "network_view": "cloud_view"
    }
]
The Response Code:200

So how come we did not see this issue before?
My guess is that NIOS had this bug and recently fixed the issue so permission is checked as mentioned in the document. However we saw that NIOS did not check superuser permission either.
We allowed the superuser on cloud api user but the API still failed. 

Anyway how can we resolve this issue now?

object_manager.delete_all_associated_objects() calls get_all_associated_objects().
This method does search using IPAddress.

obj.IPAddress.search(...)

If IP address is a fixed address, this will fail.
A funny thing is that delete_all_associated_objects() is only called from FixedAddressIPAllocator.

We probably need to make change in the interface from delete_all_associated_objects to
delete_associated_dns_records() and handle for DNS records associated the fixed address.

Also get_all_associated_objects is used by only delete_all_associated_objects.
So we need to change this method name to get_associated_dns_records.

Then how do we get DNS records from get_associated_dns_records?

Since we are not using admin credentails, we cannot use ip4addr and get dns records in one WAPI call. So we may have to do the following.

wapi/v2.3/record:a?ipv4addr=99.90.93.10
wapi/v2.3/record:aaaa?ipv6addr=2001::1    <= if ipv6 is used
wapi/v2.3/record:ptr?ipv4addr=99.90.93.10
wapi/v2.3/record:txt?ipv4addr=99.90.93.10
. . .
whateever record types defined in "dns_record_removable_types" to remove.

issue_title: return binary
issue_body:
in reference to: https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/web2py/2XOPctPRmF0
and: https://github.com/web2py/web2py/issues/1953
and directed by Massimo that it can be added to the trunk.

this would be for PostGreSQL 9 and psycopg2 currently using web2py 2.16.1.

of course this should return the binary of any blob field, not just a png, as is the example below, from postgresql wherein under version 9 its natural state is to store the binary in normal non-escaped hex, so no decoding is required, as shown below.

the essential code to reproduce would do the following:

def getbinary():
     try:
        c = psycopg2.connect("dbname='tms' host='%s' user='%s' password='%s'" % ('localhost', 'username', 'passwd'))
        q = c.cursor(cursor_factory=psycopg2.extras.DictCursor)
        q.execute('select png from png where (id = 1);')
        data = StringIO(q.fetchone()[0])
        c.commit()
    finally:
        if ('q' in locals()) and (q is not None):
            q.close()
        if ('c' in locals()) and (c is not None):
            c.close()
    response.headers['Content-Type'] = 'image/png'
    return response.stream(data, 1024)

issue_title: MXNet 0.11.0 Release Feedback: README File
issue_body:
Copied and assembled as is from this [email thread](https://lists.apache.org/thread.html/171c0ff73e5f7539611a8024fef42968f5ffea800c354b2cdfa04ab2@%3Cgeneral.incubator.apache.org%3E) 

1. Install Instructions needed - "Agreed with Justin that there needs to be a Getting Started text file of some kind. This could be a link from the README.md to the docs/get_started directory perhaps; though .md format isn't the easiest to read (ie: link to website and over time we should consider whether a local version is needed)."
2. Also the README has a large number of links to http://mxnet.io. These should be changed.
3. R-package/ directory is empty (needs removing as confusing, or a README could be added explaining the code is not present and can be found outside of Apache).
4. The README.md also refers to rc3. It shouldn't refer to rc3 as a release, and ideally it would refer to 0.11.0 as a release (though tricky to be forward looking given that GitHub treats that as a homepage). Randomly noting that the What's New should be dated.
5. The README.md refers to the copyright being owned by Contributors. Needs updating to a license statement (with NOTICE handling the copyright side of things).

issue_title: Add paging to the WebUI (or any other methods)
issue_body:
We should investigate how to remove the 100 calculation limit in the WebUI main page. We added this limit because updating (in async, every 5s) a list of more that 100 calculations is very CPU intensive on the browser side.

To remove this limitation we should add paging (aka subdivide the list in N pages with 50? calculations each); in this way the user can access all the computations but the browser will load only a subset of them.

@hascar proposed also to try with the continue loading. This may also work, but we must make sure that only the visible calculation are refreshed, otherwise after some scroll we'll have again more than hundreds calculation to refresh.

To easily test this issue I would suggest to:
- Run in batch (via `oq engine --run`) 300 times the `ScenarioCase1` demo (which is _very_ fast)
- Remove the default 100 limit in the query/view
- Use Firefox (Chrome has a faster JS engine and is less overkilled by the number of calcs)

issue_title: Exceptions can't be caught with try/except
issue_body:
Whenever I get an exception in boto which I want to catch, I copy and paste the exception code into the `except` block, and it normally fails because python doesn't know which exception I'm trying to catch.

# Steps to reproduce

Run the following code:

````
import botocore
import boto3


client = boto3.client('s3')

b = 'somebucketnamewhichdoesntexist'

response = client.get_bucket_location(
    Bucket=b
)
````

Because that bucket doesn't exist, boto raises a `botocore.errorfactory.NoSuchBucket` exception.

So copy and paste `botocore.errorfactory.NoSuchBucket` into an except block.

````
import botocore
import boto3


client = boto3.client('s3')

b = 'somebucketnamewhichdoesntexist'

try:
    response = client.get_bucket_location(
        Bucket=b
    )

issue_title: ECS services_stable waiter does not await load balance registration.
issue_body:
The services_stable waiter succeeds when the number of deployments is 1 and the runningCount matches the desiredCount. Those conditions are satisfied before a container instance is registered with a load balancer (for services having a load balancer).

```json

    "ServicesStable": {
      "delay": 15,
      "operation": "DescribeServices",
      "maxAttempts": 40,
      "acceptors": [
        {
          "expected": "MISSING",
          "matcher": "pathAny",
          "state": "failure",
          "argument": "failures[].reason"
        },
        {
          "expected": "DRAINING",
          "matcher": "pathAny",
          "state": "failure",
          "argument": "services[].status"
        },
        {
          "expected": "INACTIVE",
          "matcher": "pathAny",
          "state": "failure",
          "argument": "services[].status"
        },
        {
          "expected": true,
          "matcher": "path",
          "state": "success",
          "argument": "services | [@[?length(deployments)!=`1`], @[?desiredCount!=runningCount]][] | length(@) == `0`"
        }
      ]
    }
```

I do not see any formal attribute that indicates when an ECS Service with a load balancer stabilized, but the first event message will end with `has reached a steady state.` following successful registration.

Can we check the event message with the jmespath expression to determine when the service has stabilized?

I found this expression produces the expected result:

```
services | [@[?length(deployments)!=`1`], @[?desiredCount!=runningCount], @[?!ends_with(events[0].message, 'reached a steady state.')]][] | length(@) == `0`
```

I suppose checking the event message actually obviates the need for the first two checks, so it could be simplified as:

```
services[?!ends_with(events[0].message, 'reached a steady state.')][] | length(@) == `0`
```

If checking the event message in the waiter is unacceptable, do you have any suggestions on how to use the ECS / ELB APIs to make this check?

issue_title: Don't allow session with bad services or regions.
issue_body:
Boto should not allow you to create a session with a service and region combination that is not available.

For example, if I create a redshift session in us-west-1, boto allows me to create this session and then takes quite a while to fail my requests. I'm seeing response times in excess of 10 seconds for boto to raise an  `EndpointConnectionError`. I see the same thing when using the AWS CLI:

```
$ time aws --region=us-west-1 redshift describe-clusters

Could not connect to the endpoint URL: "https://redshift.us-west-1.amazonaws.com/"

real	0m11.699s
user	0m0.272s
sys	0m0.036s
```

It looks like a list of available endpoints is kept in the tests [here](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/80b0161316dbbe355149f619da4ecf1565123e93/tests/unit/test_regions.py#L27).


Ideally boto would raise an error when a user attempts to even create the session. But if that does not sound reasonable, it would be nice to at least have formal access to that `KNOWN_REGIONS` list so that I can perform the validation myself.

This is not an issue with EC2 because it provides the `describe_regions` method. Most services don't seem to provide that.

issue_title: automagic application header propagation
issue_body:
@angiez00 encountered a bug where the developer had assumed application headers would be forwarded to all downstream calls, when in reality they are not.

The expectation here is
* You get an inbound HTTP request with some headers. Some of those headers *always* (or almost always) need to be included in downstream tchannel requests (as application headers).
* You get an inbound tchannel request. Same story. Some of those request headers always need to propagated downstream.

The obvious use cases here are authn/z.

I don't think we can blindly forward all headers, that's too error-prone and probably overkill. Instead I think we can give developers a way to configure tchannel to always forward the provided list of headers. We can add hooks for HTTP if there's a strong need for that.

@abhinav @breerly @junchaowu 

cc @Raynos @prashantv for perspectives from other languages. AFAIK node/go have context objects that get passed everywhere, and you could copy the request headers to that object for downstream calls?

issue_title: Support aliasses like 'workon', 'cdsitepackages', 'cdproject', 'cdvirtualenv'
issue_body:
I'm trying to switch from virtualenvwrapper to pipenv, and find I really miss the aliasses. I know I can use them together, but I find that I would really like to leave virtualenvwrapper behind (for various reasons).

What I'm missing most is support for `workon`, `cdsitepackages`, `cdproject`. Is there any chance these can be included as subcommands in pipenv? To be more precise, I think `cdsitepackages`, `cdproject` could/ should still be aliasses once you have `pipenv shell` . But especially workon is something im dearly missing.

issue_title: Trouble installing editable packages from git repos
issue_body:
# Issue

I'm having trouble getting pipenv to deal with editable installs from git. I've narrowed down as much as possible for the purposes of this test. There's a [repo here](https://github.com/drcongo/pipenvtest) with the whole thing for reproducing.
```
-e git+http://github.com/hactar-is/django-admin-list-filter-dropdown.git@1a5e6040dfeca6b09317401f5bd7a518141c9433#egg=django_admin_list_filter_dropdown
-e git+git@codebasehq.com:hactar/hactar-library/wagtail_eventbrite.git@90bdfc680fc920eed8618d29aefac770d258c359#egg=wagtail_eventbrite
-e git://github.com/miracle2k/webassets.git@cc440911c6ac5f08fd98ea08442fe06e7a0aea2a#egg=webassets
```

When I run `pipenv install` I get this output:
```
requirements.txt found, instead of Pipfile! Converting…
Warning: Your Pipfile now contains pinned versions, if your requirements.txt did.
We recommend updating your Pipfile to specify the "*" version, instead.
Pipfile.lock not found, creating…
Locking [dev-packages] dependencies…
Locking [packages] dependencies…
nv/utils.py", line 285, in actually_resolve_reps
    dep[len('-e '):]
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/pipenv/vendor/pip9/req/req_install.py", line 144, in from_editable
    editable_req, default_vcs)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/pipenv/vendor/pip9/req/req_install.py", line 1197, in parse_editable
    "Could not detect requirement name, please specify one with #egg="
pip9.exceptions.InstallationError: Could not detect requirement name, please specify one with #egg=
```

Obviously the Pipfile.lock doesn't get created but the Pipfile does, and that contains this...
```
[[source]]
url = "https://pypi.python.org/simple"
verify_ssl = true
name = "pypi"

[dev-packages]

[requires]
python_version = "3.5"

[packages]
"4c58dfa" = {path = "git+http://github.com/hactar-is/django-admin-list-filter-dropdown.git@1a5e6040dfeca6b09317401f5bd7a518141c9433", editable = true}
wagtail_eventbrite = {ref = "90bdfc680fc920eed8618d29aefac770d258c359", editable = true, git = "git@codebasehq.com:hactar/hactar-library/wagtail_eventbrite.git"}
webassets = {ref = "cc440911c6ac5f08fd98ea08442fe06e7a0aea2a", editable = true, git = "git://github.com/miracle2k/webassets.git"}
```

All the #egg= bits have been removed. So I try manually adding them to the Pipfile, relevant lines now look like this:
```
"4c58dfa" = {path = "git+http://github.com/hactar-is/django-admin-list-filter-dropdown.git@1a5e6040dfeca6b09317401f5bd7a518141c9433#egg=django_admin_list_filter_dropdown", editable = true}
wagtail_eventbrite = {ref = "90bdfc680fc920eed8618d29aefac770d258c359", editable = true, git = "git@codebasehq.com:hactar/hactar-library/wagtail_eventbrite.git#egg=wagtail_eventbrite"}
webassets = {ref = "cc440911c6ac5f08fd98ea08442fe06e7a0aea2a", editable = true, git = "git://github.com/miracle2k/webassets.git#egg=webassets"}
```

`pipenv lock` produces this:
```
Locking [dev-packages] dependencies…
Locking [packages] dependencies…

issue_title: Public API to get EnvironmentInfo for a compiler
issue_body:
At numpy, we have used the following to locate the "redist" directory so that we can copy DLLs into our wheel. However, the code is quite fragile. It would be better if setuptools would provide a public API that would allow us to get an instance of environmentinfo for an MSVCCompiler object.

```python
# Defer import of setuptools to avoid
 +            # runtime effects when not needed
 +            from setuptools import msvc
 +
 +            # Initialize to get compiler.cc
 +            if not self.compiler.initialized:
 +                self.compiler.initialize()
 +
 +            # The 'bin' directory
 +            # Paths on windows are not case-sensitive
 +            VCTools = os.path.normcase(
 +                os.path.dirname(self.compiler.cc))
 +            PLAT_TO_VCVARS = {
 +                'win32' : 'x86',
 +                'win-amd64' : 'x86_amd64',
 +            }
 +            
 +            print('Trying to find MSVC version for: ' + VCTools)
 +            # I attempt to avoid implementation
 +            # details as much as possible. Hopefully
 +            # this will work some time before
 +            # needing adjustment.
 +            ei = msvc.EnvironmentInfo(
 +                PLAT_TO_VCVARS[get_platform()])
 +            for version in list(ei.si.find_available_vc_vers()):
 +                ei = msvc.EnvironmentInfo(
 +                    PLAT_TO_VCVARS[get_platform()],
 +                    vc_ver=version)
 +                print('Trying version: ' + str(version))
 +                directories = [
 +                    os.path.normcase(d) for d in ei.VCTools
 +                ]
 +                directories += [
 +                    d.replace('x86_amd64', 'amd64')
 +                    for d in list(directories)
 +                ]
 +                for directory in directories:
 +                    print('Trying: ' + directory)
 +                    if directory == VCTools:
 +                        break
 +                else:
 +                    continue
 +                break
 +            else:
 +                raise ValueError('Unable to find a'
 +                                 'matching MSVC version')
 +
 +            redist_dir = os.path.dirname(
 +                ei.VCRuntimeRedist)
```

issue_title: make flexible tooltips for LZ
issue_body:
### different sets of attributes for each phenotype

LocusZoom requires me to specify which attributes will exist (`layout.panels[#id=association].data_layers[#id=associationpvalues].fields`).  But I want users to be able to use whichever attributes they want.  For now, I'll have a constant set of attributes for each pheweb, but later I'll likely have a different set for each phenotype _within_ a pheweb.

I'd like to skip straight to dynamically setting `fields` for each phenotype.

Options:

1. Know which attributes we have before we serve our javascript, and modify it server-side.

2. On the client, modify things after receiving data.  `.getData(...)` is probably the best place to do that.  Chris didn't like that idea, and I didn't understand why not.  Look into `parseResponse`, especially the one in `GeneSource`.

### tooltips

LocusZoom tooltip templating only allows substituting values, and nothing more complicated.  Different phewebs have different attributes to show.  Soon even different phenotypes within the same pheweb will have different attributes. Later, within a single phenotype, different variants will have different attributes.  (eg, GWAS Catalog hits).  On the LZ-PheWAS, different points have different attributes.

I'd love to just have a function that takes a variant and returns HTML.  What if the tooltip just included an id and `<script>foo(variant)</script>`?

### Conversation with Chris:
-    // just follow the docs at https://github.com/statgen/locuszoom
-    // plot.on('data_rendered', () => { modify this.**.tooltip })
-    // but .fields is gonna be a problem
-    // I could hackishly modify it in the .getData
-    // or I can make the parseResponse &c act more forgiving like in GeneSource.

issue_title: api: Implement compose_objects just like minio-go
issue_body:
High-level copy_object requirements:

- [ ] Support copying objects of all sizes.
- [ ] Support source objects with an arbitrary range header (i.e. any valid start and end offset of a source object) via multipart copy object.
- [ ] Support all copy conditions (this is already supported).

Currently, the library supports only copying objects <= 5GiB in size. Larger objects can also be copied via a multipart-copy-object strategy.

The multipart copy object operation consists of starting a new multipart upload, followed by 1 or more copy-object-part requests, and finally a complete-multipart request.

Note that copy object via a single PUT request does not support range headers, but copy-object-part does support this.

This feature was recently implemented in the [Haskell SDK](https://github.com/minio/minio-hs/commit/e4e2576c7488878661eaa2d7fa68ab12e177d094).

issue_title: Deduplicate docstrings
issue_body: There are a number of kwarg docstrings that are duplicated, especially across the various consumer classes. We should find a way to deduplicate these while still allowing the documentation generator to see them.

issue_title: Handle when components are defined inside topology module
issue_body: I recently rediscovered that you cannot define a `Bolt` or `Spout` inside a topology module, because the topology module does not get packaged up with the the rest of the code. The error message you get is really cryptic, because the Storm workers will crash when launching the process telling you the topology module cannot be found. We should either explicitly detect and disallow this behavior, or add the topology file to the JAR that gets created. (Personally, I think adding the Python file to the JAR would be difficult, because the import paths in the topology module itself are unlikely to still work.)

issue_title: Allow non-uberjar deploys for speed & simplicity
issue_body:
We currently have `lein uberjar` running in these lines:

https://github.com/Parsely/streamparse/blob/master/streamparse/ext/invoke.py#L135-L146

That makes the JAR file contain Storm + all its JAR dependencies, which explodes the topology JAR size and makes deploys slow. A Python codebase that is only 20-30KB ends up being a 15MB JAR to submit.

issue_title: update dependencies
issue_body:
In https://github.com/cs50/submit50/blob/master/Makefile:

* add `python3`
* remove `expect`, `jq`, `util-linux` (I think last is safe to remove now)

Can the Python dependencies in `requirements.txt` be installed as `apt-get` dependencies? E.g., for dependency `foo`, does `python3-foo` exist, so we can avoid calling `pip3` in `after-install.sh`, per http://askubuntu.com/questions/327543/how-can-a-debian-package-install-python-modules-from-pypi?

Either way, `requirements.txt` shouldn't end up in `bin`. Ideally should be integrated into `Makefile` or `after-install.sh`.

issue_title: RFC: PostgreSQL support, jsonalchemy query object
issue_body:
*See also the footer for a second issue.*

## Improving queries on PostgreSQL records.

With the introduction of version 9.4 PostgreSQL allows using binary representation of JSON as a field - it is called `jsonb` and it is supported by SqlAlchemy. Obviously its performance is much better than `json`'s.

http://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.4/static/functions-json.html
http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/rel_0_9/dialects/postgresql.html#sqlalchemy.dialects.postgresql.JSONB

Moreover, after indexing such structures with GIN indices they work reasonably well. For example let us consider table author_jsonb:

id | json
---|------
integer| jsonb

And an example structure:

```python
{
    'papers' = [{'recid': 1 },{'recid': 2}],
    'canonical_name' = 'some_string'
}
```
Simple queries are very effective!
``` sql
select id from author_jsonb where json @> '{"canonical_name" : "some_string"}';
```
My benchmarks show that we might expect maximum 10% performance drop on such queries compared to relational databases. However, the more complicated queries we create, the significantly worse performance is expected (much worse than in relational version of database). 

``` sql
create index papers_index_b on author_jsonb using gin (((json->'papers')) jsonb_path_ops);
select id from author_jsonb where ((json->'papers')) @> '[{"recid" : 2}]';
```
Moreover, they require querying for the whole key/value pair - for example querying for an existance of a key with `?` operator is very expensive, no matter how we index the table.

Fortunately, it seems that there are people who work on improving this (see attached files):
http://www.pgcon.org/2014/schedule/events/696.en.html

They introduce a query language for `jsonb` field called Jsquery. Now my query might look like this:

```sql
select id from author_jsonb @@ 'papers @> {"recid": 2}';
```
This language is supposed to support key existance searches, querying for nested values and array operations better than current `jsonb` operators. Here is the link to the [official development repo](https://github.com/akorotkov/jsquery).

I believe this is a technology our project might want to use. We can also contribute in its development by suggesting weak points (if we are able to find some). Obviously, it will take some time for SqlAlchemy developers to provide this in their tool, so it is a glance into the future.

## Storage engines

From the main page of our readthedocs:

>Invenio requires a relational database backend to store information. MySQL or PostgreSQL (coming soon) are required for basic functionality, but there’s also support for a MongoDB of other experimental NoSQL solutions, including using SQLite for local development.

What is in current plans for invenio? If we are going to support configurable storage engines can you tell me more details about what will be available, how will it be reflected on our database, etc.?

issue_title: App crashes with aws boto3
issue_body:
I am creating a simple Kivy app that accesses an aws Dynamodb table. My test app runs fine locally from the command line (returns data without errors) but when I build and run on an Android device using Buildozer the app compiles and deploys but crashes as soon I try to communicate with aws.

adb logcat shows this:

`03-07 12:39:25.570 10601 10825 I python  :  ValueError: Expecting , delimiter: line 70 column 29 (char 1787)`
`03-07 12:39:25.625 10601 10825 I python  : Python for android ended.`

The line my app fails on is this:

`dynamodb = boto3.resource('dynamodb',a_access_key_id = 'AKImy keyH6Q',aws_secret_access_key = 'nl4ymSnZ9my keyecx',region_name = 'eu-west-1')`

My buildozer.spec file contains these requirements:

# (list) Permissions
android.permissions = INTERNET

# (list) Application requirements
requirements = kivy,boto3,botocore,python-dateutil,jmespath,openssl,futures,pyjnius, android

issue_title: The SDL2 bootstrap can't make a Kivy Launcher
issue_body:
As in the title, the SDL2 bootstrap doesn't have any code to make a Kivy Launcher APK. It should be quite easy to add it just by looking at the way the pygame bootstrap does it, but the work needs doing.

The launcher could also be a separate bootstrap, but there might then be a lot of code duplication. Maybe there is another way to resolve this though.

issue_title: QUANDL: URL format need to be updated.
issue_body:
Hi

Located in quandl.py line 86:
`url = '{url}{dataset}/{symbol}.csv?{params}'`
This line should be changed to:
`url = '{url}{dataset}/{symbol}/data.csv?{params}'`

Otherwise you will get error from quandl:
Response Text:
b'code,message\nQECx02,You have submitted an incorrect Quandl code. Please check your Quandl codes and try again.\n'

issue_title: Google Daily Reader broken, URL dead

issue_title: libbrotli
issue_body:
Hey,

I created the sub project libbrotli (https://github.com/bagder/libbrotli) a while ago to help build and install a _library_ for brotli encoding/decoding, using only code from this repository. libbrotli is only a meta-project with mostly autotools to build, install and package a "library" for brotli since this original brotli home does not provide that. It only uses compression/decompression source code from the brotli tree.

This concept seems to resonate with a decent amount of users who appreciate being able to get a library (or two actually) out of a build and install process for use in various projects.

I would prefer if this functionality was provided by the brotli project itself and I'll offer to merge/translate it over to a pull-request or something if you'll agree this is interesting. I'd prefer to remove myself as a middle man here.

issue_title: Use the _W versions of the Windows API, especially for SEC_WINNT_AUTH_IDENTITY
issue_body:
It would be much prefered if you used the _W versions of the API, especially for the  auth identify.

Both usernames and credentials/passwords might contain UTF-16 chars outside of the current codepage.

In addition, your code reacts in funny ways if one changes the current user local (e.g. when using remote desktop, try switching between turkish, german and EN_US and use some umlauts or accents in the usernames or passwords, those might get silently converted).

Its close to trivial to use the UTF-16 APIs from python thanks to the usual unicode support.

issue_title: evm: detectors should generate testcase
issue_body:
evm detectors are plugins that detect potential security concerns in an explored path. currently they trigger output in the workspace and stdout. they should also generate a testcase which can be used to trigger the specific issue detected.

example: we currently have an int overflow detector. it will print a warning when a path executes a potential int overflow. the testcase generated for that path will not necessarily be one that triggers the overflow, it will simply execute that path. we should also generate a testcase with inputs that explicitly trigger the overflow.

putting this in 0.1.6 milestone, unclear if we'll get to it in time

issue_title: Update logging.list_entries to use the `resourceNames` field
issue_body:
It looks like `projectIds` was deprecated -- not sure when, but appears so (see https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/api/reference/rest/v2/entries/list). It'll still work, so we're not in any major time crunch here, but we should swap out to using the `resourceNames` field when sending stuff to the API.

Ideally, this method would accept both `projects`, `organizations`, and generic `resources`. `projects` would just be prefixed with `projects/`, same for organizations, and resources just appended to the list of resource names where we assume that the person understands the format.

If we're going to swap the order of the parameters (ie, put `organizations` after `projects`) then this might be considered a breaking change :(

issue_title: Allow logging metrics to be created with LogEntry V1 syntax
issue_body:
At present, it is [only possible to create a new Log metric with V2 syntax](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/logging/google/cloud/logging/metric.py#L41).

The [default behaviour of the API is to use version="V2" when version is not specified](https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/api/reference/rest/Shared.Types/LogMetric).

As a result, a user who tries to create a metric using the old syntax will encounter an error:

```python
from google.cloud import logging
client = logging.Client(project='my-example-project')
metric = client.metric('my-example-metric', 'metadata.serviceName="compute.googleapis.com"', 'All GCE logs')
metric.create()
```

```
ValueError: RPC failed: <_Rendezvous of RPC that terminated with (StatusCode.INVALID_ARGUMENT, Field not found: 'metadata'.)>
```

Given that this behaviour persists with the [API Explorer](https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/api/reference/rest/v2/projects.metrics/create) when not specifying version, the cause is that my filter is in V1 syntax, but the server expects V2.

This is made more confusing as the Cloud Console [still uses the V1 syntax](https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/api/v2/migration-to-v2#logs_viewer), so a user who is trying to copy a filter they have crafted in the Console will be greeted by this error when using `google-cloud-python`.

`google-cloud-python` should add the parameter `version` to the `Metric` class in order to resolve this issue.

Workarounds:
- [Migrate filters to V2 syntax.](https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/api/v2/migration-to-v2#v2-logentry-changes)
- Use `google-apis-client-python` instead of `google-cloud-python`.

Package version:
- google-cloud==0.20.0
- google-cloud-logging==0.20.0

issue_title: Best Practices for running in a CI/test environment?
issue_body:
I have an appengine project I'm migrating to Google Cloud Datastore. The appengine libraries set up a stub for me with `init_datastore_v3_stub`. But with `datastore`, it's trying to connect to the GCE project (os.environ['APPLICATION_ID'] et al).

This describes setting up prod credentials, which I do not want: https://cloud.google.com/solutions/continuous-delivery-with-travis-ci (I don't want tests writing to prod)

My options as I see them:
- setup a global "test project" on GCE, and include private credentials for it. Wouldn't really scale well to multiple people operating on the same DB simultaneously...but would work fine for my solo-development purposes
- set up a stub/dev-server with https://cloud.google.com/datastore/docs/tools/datastore-emulator from within the travis config/tests. Has anyone done this?

I found this searching on github:
https://github.com/jens-meiss/blog/blob/afd48268ace7ab607bbd0c0a2368dda0ca1fdf7d/.travis.yml

It appears to authorize as a test account/project *and* set up an emulator datastore, and I'm unclear why it does both. I'd love a best-practices doc on how to use this stuff from a CI/testing framework!
